B: RV & LV walls come back to normal size

Document [0] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Histopathology) Histological examination of the vasculature in Eisenmenger syndrome would resemble other forms of pulmonary hypertension. Increased proliferation of vascular smooth muscle, evidence of lung parenchymal scarring (pulmonary fibrosis), and decreased luminal caliber due to cellular proliferation in the vascular walls all are indicative of a PAH process.
Document [1] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes) Eisenmenger syndrome is best managed by an interprofessional team that includes a cardiologist, pulmonologist, cardiac surgeon, internist, thoracic surgeon, and intensivist. The disorder has many causes, and the key is to treat the primary disorder. Once Eisenmenger syndrome develops, the prognosis is poor. Heart and lung transplants are an option, but the lack of donors is a problem. Medications to dilate the pulmonary vasculature are unreliable or consistent in efficacy. Most patients are dead within 12 to 24 months after the diagnosis is made. [9] [10] [11]
Document [2] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity) Eisenmenger syndrome (ES) is a constellation of symptoms that arise from a congenital heart defect and result in large anatomic shunts. Due to anatomic variations present at birth, hemodynamic forces initially result in a left-right shunt, which develops into severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and elevated vascular resistance. Ultimately, due to increased pulmonary vascular resistance, the left-to-right shunt will become a right-to-left shunt, resulting in significant hypoxemia and cyanosis. This activity discusses the evaluation and treatment of Eisenmenger syndrome and further defines the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.
Document [3] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Treatment / Management) Cardiopulmonary transplantation is curative for Eisenmenger syndrome. However, this is impractical in most settings. Pharmacology has improved symptoms but not mortality. Potential pharmaceuticals for the treatment of Eisenmenger syndrome include diuretics, antiarrhythmics, and anticoagulation in some patients. Supplemental oxygen has not definitively shown to have a mortality benefit. Vasodilator therapies may provide an opportunity for clinical research, and studies have shown some symptomatic improvement. The Bosentan Randomized Trial of Endothelin Antagonist Therapy-5 trial showed improved exercise capacity and symptomatic improvement with endothelin antagonists in patients with ASD, VSD, and PDA with Eisenmenger syndrome [1] . Subsequent studies show mortality benefits with sildenafil as well. Warfarin has been classically used for anticoagulation in Eisenmenger syndrome. In theory, anticoagulation may dispel some of the pathology related to in situ thrombosis inherent with the disease. However, the evidence for this practice is based on mostly observational studies.
Document [4] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Evaluation) All patients with suspicion for Eisenmenger syndrome should undergo a detailed history and physical, with particular attention on determining triggers for PAH. Patients should also undergo an evaluation to determine the degree of PAH as well as ventricular function. As there is evidence supporting symptomatic improvement with vasodilator therapy as in PAH, patients should also undergo vasoreactivity testing. Workup should include pulse oximetry, chest radiograph, EKG, pulmonary function tests, complete blood count, iron studies, and cytidine monophosphate testing. Complete cardiac catheterization should occur at centers that specialize in the management of adult congenital heart disease and PAH. Loud murmurs may not be present in Eisenmenger syndrome. In such cases, left ventricle pressure may be similar to right ventricle pressure, creating a very small gradient. Without turbulence, there is no readily appreciable murmur on auscultation.
Document [5] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Pathophysiology) The most common defects leading to Eisenmenger syndrome are ASD, VSD, and PDA defects. The following 3 main processes result in the ultimate reversal of a left-to-right into a right-to-left shunt: Vasoconstriction, which is due to imbalances in pulmonary vascular tone, followed by Vascular remodeling, which is due to the proliferation of pulmonary vascular smooth muscle, and finally Thrombosis, which is caused by the increased resistance of blood flow.
Document [6] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Introduction) Eisenmenger syndrome is a constellation of symptoms that arise from a congenital heart defect and result in large anatomic shunts. Due to anatomic variations present at birth, hemodynamic forces initially result in a left-right shunt, which develops into severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (PAH) and elevated vascular resistance. Ultimately, due to increased pulmonary vascular resistance, the left-to-right shunt becomes a right-to-left shunt, resulting in significant hypoxemia and cyanosis. PAH is a mean pulmonary arterial pressure greater than 25 mmHg while resting or 30 mmHg when exercising. This may occur in large shunts or complex, unrepaired congenital heart disease as early as the first decade of life.
Document [7] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Etiology) Any heart defect that leads to the development of PAH can cause Eisenmenger syndrome. This is commonly seen in atrial septal defects (ASD), ventricular septal defects (VSD), atrioventricular septal defects, and patent ductus arteriosus (PDA), but it can occur in more complex lesions as well. Unrepaired Tetralogy of Fallot may also result in Eisenmenger syndrome. Without early repair, reversal of a left-to-right shunt may result in a bidirectional or right-to-left shunt. The subsequent hypoxemia due to this shunt may not be responsive to oxygen.
Document [8] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Pearls and Other Issues) Key facts to keep in mind about Eisenmenger syndrome include: Loud murmurs may not be present in severe Eisenmenger syndrome due to the lack of a pressure gradient between ventricles and, therefore, low turbulence that would otherwise produce them. Hypoxemia that is responsive to oxygen therapy should be treated. In women who develop PAH with CHD, pregnancy is contraindicated.
Document [9] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Treatment / Management) Clinical follow-up should focus on checking annual CBC, iron studies, kidney function, and uric acid, with a focus on correcting any abnormalities. Patients also should be assessed with pulse oximetry, both with and without supplemental O2. Any abnormalities suggesting hypoxemia warrant further evaluation. Surgical correction of the causative heart defect in adult patients is generally contraindicated [2] . In patients who have developed PAH as a consequence of unrepaired CHD, the defect itself may be acting as a protective measure, preventing worsening of the pulmonary vascular resistance in the face of increasing right ventricular pressure [3] . In children, the development of pulmonary vascular disease (greater than 6 Woods units/m2) with poor vasodilator response is likely to face post-surgical complications, including right heart failure, increased pulmonary hypertension, and hypertensive crises. Classically, it is thought to be of little benefit to undergo such risky procedures with a plethora of potential consequences. Additionally, even with clinical response to vasodilator therapies for the pulmonary arterial hypertension component, there are only a select few case reports that show benefit for the closure of the anatomical defect. However, there is promising evidence that cardiac surgery and close peri- and post-operational management of pulmonary vascular resistance with advanced PAH therapies may improve safety and mitigate downstream problems. [4] [5] [6] [7] [8]
Document [10] (Title: Embryology, Heart -- Pathophysiology) The term shunt refers to abnormal communication between vascular structures, and these shunts permit non-physiologic blood flow along pressure gradients. Blood flowing from the right side of the heart to the left side is termed a “right-to-left” shunt, while blood flowing from the left side of the heart to the right side is a “left-to-right” shunt. Right-to-left shunt implies the circulation of deoxygenated blood (i.e., blood that has yet to reach the pulmonary system) to the systemic circulation. Patients with a right to left shunts present with cyanosis and at increased risk for paradoxical embolism and the associated complications like infarction and abscess formation. In contrast, left-to-right shunts do not present with cyanosis since the systemic circulation still receives oxygenated blood. However, the increase in pulmonary blood load from the left-to-right shunt leads to profound pathological responses, partly due to the pulmonary system being a “low-pressure” system incapable of withstanding the increased pressure.The pulmonary arteries typically respond to the increased blood flow and pressure via hypertrophy and vasoconstriction. Eventually, pulmonary vascular resistance approaches systemic levels, creating a shunt reversal (now right-to-left) to distribute deoxygenated blood into the systemic system. The name given this condition of shunt reversal is Eisenmenger syndrome. Once pulmonary hypertension develops, the congenital heart condition progresses beyond irreparability.
Document [11] (Title: Atrial Septal Defect -- Pathophysiology) The smooth muscle layer in the vascular wall thickens as the pulmonary vessels remodel. This increase in muscle mass raises the resistance to flow in the pulmonary circuit. Pulmonary hypertension develops due to the rise in vascular resistance and, subsequently, pulmonary pressures. When pulmonary pressures equal systemic pressures, the shunt across the ASD reverses, allowing deoxygenated blood to flow into the left atrium and, ultimately, the systemic circulation. This reversal of the shunt due to pulmonary hypertension leads to Eisenmenger syndrome.
Document [12] (Title: Truncus Arteriosus -- Pathophysiology) The mixing of pulmonary and systemic blood and variations in pulmonary blood flow and PVR initiate the pathophysiologic mechanisms of truncus arteriosus. PVR is relatively high starting the 1st week of life. Shunting of mixed oxygenated and deoxygenated blood into the systemic circuit increases, causing mild cyanosis. Pulmonary overcirculation causes congestive heart failure as PVR drops and PBF rises. If untreated, pulmonary overcirculation eventually leads to irreversible pulmonary vascular disease with fixed pulmonary hypertension or Eisenmenger syndrome. [20] [21]
Document [13] (Title: Aortopulmonary Septal Defect -- Prognosis) The prognosis for an isolated AP window is good when treated early with surgical closure and avoidance of the development of pulmonary vascular disease. [24] Late diagnosis can be associated with the development of pulmonary hypertension/Eisenmenger syndrome, which carries a poor prognosis. An associated AP window with other congenital heart lesions increases the complexity of the surgery. [23] [26]
Document [14] (Title: Pulmonary Hypertension -- Etiology) Pulmonary hypertension associated with CHD (PH-CHD) is classified into CHD that causes Eisenmenger syndrome, PAH associated with prevalent systemic-to-pulmonary shunts, PAH with small or coincidental defects, and PAH after defect correction. [10] While PH-CHD is predominantly classified under group 1 pulmonary hypertension, some defects are more appropriately classified under group 2. These conditions include mitral valve disease, left ventricular inflow or outflow obstruction, and left ventricular systolic or diastolic dysfunction that may also result in postcapillary pulmonary hypertension.

Identify patients considered high risk for Eisenmenger syndrome Evaluate the physical exam findings associated with Eisenmenger syndrome. Communicate the importance of improving care coordination amongst the interprofessional team to enhance care delivery for patients with Eisenmenger syndrome. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.
Document [16] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Prognosis) Patients with Eisenmenger syndrome have reduced life expectancies, with high mortality in the third and fourth decades. Ventricular failure, hemoptysis, pregnancy complications, and strokes are common causes of death.
Document [17] (Title: Eisenmenger Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis) Due to similar presentation and resemblances of the underlying pathophysiology, other causes of pulmonary hypertension should be ruled out. Certain rheumatological and autoimmune diseases, such as mixed connective tissue disorder, scleroderma, and systemic lupus erythematosus, may have similar presentations. Hepatitis B, C, and HIV serologies also must be considered, as these may have systemic vascular presentations.
Document [18] (Title: Lutembacher Syndrome -- Evaluation) The specific electrocardiographic (ECG) findings are tall and peaked P waves in the leads II, III, and avF and a deep prolonged terminal negative deflection of P wave in the lead V1, suggestive of biatrial enlargement. QRS shows right axis deviation, right ventricular hypertrophy (RVH), and complete or incomplete right bundle branch block. RVH and atrial fibrillation are disproportionately more common in LS when compared to an isolated ASD. Chest x-rays reveal enlarged RA, RV and main pulmonary artery with increased end-on pulmonary vascular markings suggesting increased left to right shunt. Pulmonary venous congestion is usually absent unless the ASD is restrictive. Two-dimensional echocardiography with color flow Doppler establishes the diagnosis of LS. [6] The severity of MS and the size and type of ASD are accurately estimated. Gradient across the mitral valve is less despite severe MS. Doppler pressure half-time usually overestimates the mitral valve orifice area because the ASD decompresses the LA and thus decreases the transmitral gradient. Planimetry is the more reliable method to assess the severity of MS in patients with LS. Transesophageal echocardiography outlines the site and size of ASD with its flow pattern. Cardiac catheterization is seldom required to make the diagnosis of LS in the current era. Physicians are sometimes required to assess the reversibility of PAH, measure the mitral valve area, and evaluate the coronary anatomy in high-risk patients.
Document [19] (Title: Anatomy, Thorax, Heart Ductus Arteriosus -- Clinical Significance -- Patent Ductus Arteriosus) Patent ductus arteriosus (PDA) is a condition in which the ductus arteriosus fails to close after birth. A PDA is one of the most common cardiac defects associated with congenital rubella syndrome and may severely impact an infant's quality of life. [9] The PDA leads to the generation of a left-to-right shunt since the blood pressures in the aorta are much higher than in the pulmonary artery. If allowed to remain patent, the pressure inside the pulmonary circuit will rise, leading to pulmonary hypertension. Failure to adequately address this pulmonary hypertension may lead to cyanosis and shunt reversal (Eisenmenger syndrome). Late complications of Eisenmenger syndrome include arrhythmias and sudden cardiac death. The development of this syndrome indicates irreversible changes to the cardiac and pulmonary vasculature, and lung/cardiac transplants are the only viable treatment option. [10]
Document [20] (Title: Aortopulmonary Septal Defect -- Evaluation) Echocardiography should completely evaluate the remaining cardiac structures, including evaluating other cardiac diseases such as tetralogy of Fallot, and interruption of the aortic arch, and care should be taken to identify the coronary arteries. Unless the diagnosis is made late or cannot be made with less invasive means, cardiac catheterization adds little to the diagnostic management. When the diagnosis is made after infancy or even into adult life, especially if cyanosis is noted, catheterization could be utilized to evaluate pulmonary vascular resistance as Eisenmenger syndrome can develop. If there is pulmonary vascular disease, reactivity testing should be performed during catheterization to determine the advisability of closure. [22]
Document [21] (Title: Pulmonary Stenosis -- Pathophysiology -- Types of Pulmonary Stenosis) Subpravalvular pulmonary stenosis: Supravalvular pulmonary stenosis, also known as peripheral pulmonary stenosis, is a condition where there is an obstruction above the pulmonary valve. The restriction can occur in the main pulmonary artery, the distal branches of the pulmonary artery, or both places. Supravalvular pulmonary stenosis can be associated with structural defects such as tetralogy of Fallot, double-outlet right ventricle, transposition of the great arteries, and genetic syndromes, including Noonan, William, and Alagille syndromes. [23] [24] [25] [26] [27] [28] Supravalvular pulmonary stenosis can also occur after surgical repair of the transposition of the great arteries. [29]
Document [22] (Title: Major Aortopulmonary Collateral Arteries -- Differential Diagnosis -- Williams Syndrome) Williams syndrome involves elastin gene deletions leading to cardiovascular abnormalities, including supravalvular aortic stenosis and peripheral pulmonary stenosis. The condition can occasionally mimic the hemodynamic effects seen in MAPCAs due to the development of collateral networks. In clinical practice, evaluating a patient with suspected MAPCAs should include a thorough diagnostic workup involving echocardiography, cardiac MRI, and, often, cardiac catheterization. These studies help map the extent of the collateral network, assess the functionality of the pulmonary circulation, and distinguish MAPCAs from other differential diagnoses. Accurate identification of the underlying condition is crucial for planning appropriate surgical or interventional treatments, including unifocalization of collaterals, repair of associated defects, or, in some cases, transplantation.

Document [24] (Title: Central Aortopulmonary Shunt -- Contraindications) Irreversible pulmonary hypertension (Eisenmenger physiology): If pulmonary vascular resistance is at or above systemic levels, a central aortopulmonary shunt will not increase pulmonary flow effectively and instead can cause immediate right-to-left shunting with severe hypoxemia.

Document [26] (Title: Heart-Lung Transplantation -- Technique or Treatment -- Recipient Explantation) The recipient explantation (cardiopneumonectomy) proceeds through a median sternotomy, with careful dissection into the pericardium and bilateral pleural spaces, especially in patients with a prior thoracic surgical history. Dissection is often complicated in patients requiring heart-lung transplants, particularly those with Eisenmenger syndrome, on account of their extensive mediastinal venous collaterals. During dissection, avoiding injury to the phrenic, vagus, and recurrent laryngeal nerves is essential. Once dissection is complete, the patient is cannulated through the aorta, superior vena cava, and inferior vena cava and is placed on cardiopulmonary bypass and cooled. The heart is resected, dividing the aorta just distal to the aortic valve and the pulmonary artery to the pulmonic valve. When the right atrium is entered to divide it, some teams will divide at the level of the atrium to leave a cuff of atrial tissue around both the superior and inferior venae cavae; however, other teams will divide at the level of the cava proper, to avoid incorporating arrhythmogenic tissue in the anastomosis.
Document [27] (Title: Intracardiac Shunts -- Pathophysiology) Small defects can close by themselves with time; however, larger defects can sometimes require surgical intervention. Left to right shunts are often asymptomatic in childhood; however, symptoms can appear in adolescence and adulthood. Presentation often includes shortness of breath, exercise intolerance, and easy fatigability. In these defects, the right side of the heart receives more blood, which causes pulmonary hypertension over time in some patients. A rare complication of these shunts is Eisenmenger syndrome, in which shunt reversal occurs due to severe pulmonary hypertension to the point that right-sided pressures exceed left-sided pressures. This syndrome causes cyanosis and carries a high mortality. [7]

One more associated complexity is associated with VACTERL (vertebral defects, anal, cardiac, tracheal, esophageal, renal, and limb) syndrome. [4] Other genetic syndromes can also be associated, including Down syndrome, Holt-Oram, or Kartagener syndrome. [4] [13]
Document [29] (Title: Pulmonary Regurgitation -- Continuing Education Activity) Pulmonary regurgitation, characterized by blood flow from the pulmonary artery back into the right ventricle during diastole, presents various underlying causes like pulmonary hypertension, congenital heart defects (eg, tetralogy of Fallot), iatrogenic influences, or conditions like carcinoid syndrome or rheumatic heart disease. While asymptomatic severe cases might not require therapy, valve replacement becomes crucial for symptomatic or progressive cases, focusing on alleviating symptoms and restoring normal hemodynamics. The choice between percutaneous or surgical approaches is determined by individual patient factors, anatomy, and risk considerations, with post-replacement lifelong monitoring, especially in those with prosthetic valves, being essential to ensure optimal outcomes and prevent complications like thrombosis or endocarditis.
Document [30] (Title: Ventricular Septal Defect -- Pathophysiology -- Gerbode Defects) In prolonged, large left-to-right shunts, the pulmonary vascular endothelium undergoes irreversible changes, resulting in persistent PAH. When the pressure within the pulmonary circulation exceeds that of the systemic circulation, the shunt direction reverses, leading to a right-to-left shunt. This condition is known as Eisenmenger syndrome and is observed in approximately 10% to 15% of individuals with VSD.
Document [31] (Title: Heart-Lung Transplantation -- Indications) The most common indications (35%) for heart-lung transplants are congenital cardiac malformations causing Eisenmenger syndrome and secondary pulmonary arterial hypertension. [7] [8] [9] The secondary pulmonary disease becomes unsalvageable when the pulmonary vascular resistance remains greater than 3 to 5 Woods units despite a trial of inotropes and MCS. [8] [10] When a heart transplant alone is performed in the face of such high resistance, there is a risk of acute right heart failure. [8]

Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1936) Elevated mean arterial pressure, hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ), and reactive hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) are all classified within the broader vascular category. These conditions represent distinct manifestations of abnormal vascular pressure regulation. Elevated mean arterial pressure reflects a sustained increase in the average arterial pressure throughout the cardiac cycle. Hypertensive crisis denotes an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that can threaten organ function if not promptly treated. Primary pulmonary hypertension is a specific form of high pressure that originates in the pulmonary arteries, leading to right‑ventricular strain and potential respiratory complications. Reactive hypertension describes a secondary rise in systemic blood pressure that occurs in response to another underlying condition. Recognizing that these entities share a common vascular basis aids clinicians in assessing, differentiating, and managing patients with hypertension‑related disorders, thereby improving diagnostic accuracy and therapeutic outcomes.

Document [18] (Title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1723) The term خديج designates infants who have completed 29–30 weeks of gestation, and this developmental stage is directly linked to the occurrence of متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Consequently, babies classified as خديج are at risk for respiratory complications arising from lung immaturity, highlighting the importance of recognizing this gestational age category when assessing and managing neonatal respiratory health.
Document [19] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870) Essential hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is a vascular disorder that manifests through elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure. Clinically, essential hypertension is associated with chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) and is also linked to primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ). In classification schemes, essential hypertension maps to a hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم) and to its two major subtypes, diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي) and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). All of these entities—elevated pressure measurements, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, diastolic hypertension, and systolic hypertension—are considered children of the broader vascular system, highlighting their interconnected role in the pathophysiology and clinical management of vascular blood‑pressure disorders.
Document [20] (Title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2753) Elevated systolic blood pressure is classified as a vascular condition, and it belongs to the same category as several other high‑pressure disorders. Within the vascular domain, أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis) represents an acute, severe elevation of blood pressure that requires immediate medical intervention. ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension) denotes a chronic increase in pressure within the pulmonary arteries that occurs without an identifiable secondary cause. فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) describes a pattern in which only the systolic component of arterial pressure is elevated. Together, Elevated systolic blood pressure, hypertensive crisis, primary pulmonary hypertension, and systolic hypertension are all considered child entities of the broader Vascular classification, highlighting their shared relevance to vascular pathology and the importance of recognizing and managing these distinct forms of high blood pressure in patient care.
Document [21] (Title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1808) Primary hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الأساسي) is defined by the presence of elevated diastolic blood pressure, elevated mean arterial pressure, and elevated systolic blood pressure, each of which is a direct manifestation of this condition. Within the vascular domain, these elevated pressure measurements are considered vascular children, reflecting the systemic impact of primary hypertension on the circulatory system. Primary hypertension is also mapped to several related vascular entities: hypertensive crisis (أزمة فرط ضغط الدم), diastolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي), and systolic hypertension (فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي). In addition, primary hypertension is associated with primary pulmonary hypertension (ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ) and is clinically linked to abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية). Together, these relationships illustrate how primary hypertension not only elevates key blood pressure parameters but also predisposes patients to acute vascular emergencies, distinct hypertension subtypes, pulmonary vascular disease, and radiographic abnormalities, underscoring the importance of comprehensive vascular assessment and monitoring in patient care.
Document [22] (Title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1884) Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that this condition involves abnormal movement of the mitral valve leaflets and falls within the broader spectrum of diseases affecting cardiac valves. Recognizing mitral valve prolapse as a type of valvular heart disease is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and appropriate management of patients with cardiac valve abnormalities.
Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1724) Vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية) is a broad vascular condition that includes a specific subtype called congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري). Congenital vascular dilatation is classified as a form of valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي). This hierarchical relationship indicates that the congenital form of vascular dilatation is both a child category of general vascular dilatation and a distinct type of valvular heart disease, highlighting its relevance in the diagnosis and management of cardiac valve disorders.

Document [25] (Title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 862) دبيلة is associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, indicating that individuals with دبيلة may develop these respiratory complications. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and appropriate management of patients presenting with دبيلة.
Document [26] (Title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832) The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating that patients with coronary atherosclerosis frequently exhibit cardiac abnormalities and that the presence of a cardiac disorder may suggest underlying coronary artery disease, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.
Document [27] (Title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2881) Elevated diastolic blood pressure is recognized as a vascular disorder, indicating that it falls within the broader category of vascular conditions. Within this vascular category, several specific subtypes are identified: أزمة فرط ضغط الدم (hypertensive crisis), ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (reactive hypertension). These entities are all considered children of the Vascular classification, highlighting their interrelated nature as distinct but related forms of vascular pathology. Understanding that elevated diastolic blood pressure and its related subtypes belong to the same vascular family aids clinicians in recognizing patterns of disease, tailoring diagnostic evaluation, and implementing comprehensive management strategies for patients with vascular‑related hypertension.
Document [28] (Title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2104) The vascular domain encompasses several distinct forms of elevated blood pressure. Elevated diastolic blood pressure and elevated mean arterial pressure are recognized as specific vascular abnormalities that reflect increased pressure in the circulatory system. Within the same category, the Arabic‑named conditions ارتفاع ضغط الدم الرِئَوِيّ الأَوَّلِيّ (primary pulmonary hypertension), فرط ضغط الدم الانتيابي (diastolic hypertension), and فرط ضغط الدم الانقباضي (systolic hypertension) are also classified as vascular children. Together, these entities illustrate the range of hypertensive disorders that fall under the broader vascular classification, highlighting the importance of identifying and managing each type to maintain optimal cardiovascular health.

Document [30] (Title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1496) Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) is clinically associated with valvular heart disease (مرض الصمام القلبي), indicating that patients with valvular lesions may present with this acute coronary syndrome. Mitral valve prolapse (تدلي الصمام المترالي) occurs within the spectrum of valvular heart disease, reflecting abnormal displacement of the mitral leaflets that contributes to valve dysfunction. Congenital vascular dilatation (تمدد الأوعية الدموية الفطري) is classified as a type of valvular heart disease, highlighting that certain vascular anomalies are considered part of the broader valvular disease category. Understanding how unstable angina, mitral valve prolapse, and congenital vascular dilatation relate to valvular heart disease helps clinicians assess and manage patients with complex cardiac pathology more effectively.
Document [31] (Title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2842) Benign arteriolar nephrosclerosis and essential hypertension are clinically similar conditions, as both involve pathological changes in small arterial vessels that lead to elevated blood pressure and related end‑organ effects. Recognizing the overlap in their clinical presentations helps clinicians differentiate primary hypertension from secondary renal vascular disease, guides appropriate diagnostic evaluation, and informs management strategies aimed at controlling blood pressure and preserving kidney function.
